medications.indication_rationale: The cornerstone of management: glucose-coupled sodium co-transport restores water and electrolytes lost through diarrhoea and vomiting. Taken as small frequent sips it prevents progression to significant dehydration and an ED presentation for IV fluids.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 to 10 mL of oral rehydration solution every 5 minutes using a small spoon, titrating the volume up as tolerated

medications.indication_rationale: A single sublingual/wafer dose interrupts the vomiting cycle so that oral rehydration can begin — this was the pivotal intervention in the source note. In telehealth the AI Doctor recognises the need and arranges a prescription; it does not administer the drug.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ondansetron 4 mg sublingual wafer, single stat dose (as given in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: known long QT syndrome (not reported in this patient)

medications.indication_rationale: For the myalgia and mild fever; symptomatic relief only, as recorded in the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for myalgia and fever (observe the usual maximum daily dose on the label)

medications.indication_rationale: The patient already tried an over-the-counter anti-diarrhoeal without benefit. Anti-motility agents are not part of first-line management of acute viral gastroenteritis, where rehydration is the priority, and they should be avoided if there is any bloody stool or high fever. Recommending it as the mainstay is an error of commission.
There is no role for antibiotics in uncomplicated non-bloody viral gastroenteritis; prescribing them is an error of commission that adds harm without benefit.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rehydrate by 'micro-dosing' — take 5 to 10 mL of oral rehydration solution every 5 minutes with a small spoon rather than gulping from a glass.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Small volumes are absorbed without triggering the gastric stretch receptors that provoke reflex vomiting, so rehydration succeeds where drinking a glassful fails.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use oral rehydration solution rather than plain water alone, and reintroduce bland food gradually as the nausea settles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Plain water replaces volume but not electrolytes and risks hyponatraemia; ORS maintains electrolyte balance during ongoing losses.

patient_education_points.point: A virus is irritating the gut lining, making it flush out water as diarrhoea and reject intake as vomiting; the anti-nausea medicine blocks the vomit signal so you can start replacing the water and salts you have lost.
This is self-limiting: vomiting usually settles within 12 to 24 hours and diarrhoea may last 3 to 5 days before resolving on its own.
Use oral rehydration salts, not plain water alone, to keep your salts balanced while you are losing fluid.
Wash your hands thoroughly and often to avoid passing the virus on to others.

follow_up_plan.interval: review in about 48 hours if diarrhoea persists or hydration cannot be maintained
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: hydration status, whether oral rehydration is being tolerated, symptom resolution, and any emerging red-flag features
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: unable to keep oral rehydration solution down, worsening or localising abdominal pain, blood in the stool, marked dizziness on standing, or a substantial drop in urine output

must_include_items: Recognise a self-limiting viral gastroenteritis and manage conservatively via telehealth
Prioritise oral rehydration with oral rehydration solution rather than plain water
Offer/arrange an antiemetic to break the vomiting cycle and enable rehydration
Give explicit safety-netting for dehydration and for a surgical abdomen
Arrange conservative GP follow-up at ~48 hours
acceptable_alternatives: Telehealth follow-up instead of in-person GP review if the patient prefers and remains stable
Managing without an antiemetic if the patient is already tolerating some oral fluids
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for dehydration and surgical-abdomen red flags
Failing to advise oral rehydration solution over plain water
Failing to advise small frequent sips (micro-dosing)
errors_of_commission: Prescribing empirical antibiotics for non-bloody viral gastroenteritis
Making an anti-motility agent (loperamide) the mainstay of treatment
Advising rehydration with plain water only (hyponatraemia risk)
Over-triaging a stable patient straight to the emergency department
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires conservative telehealth management centred on oral rehydration PLUS explicit dehydration and surgical-abdomen safety-netting. Prescribing antibiotics, or over-triaging a stable patient to the ED, are errors of commission that should cost the case.
